children.value:
A crankshaft rotates, a piston oscillates, a bridge vibrates under traffic
load, an alternating voltage drives a motor. Periodic phenomena are everywhere
in mechanical engineering, and the mathematical tool for describing all of them
is the same: the trigonometric functions. In this section we build these
functions from the ground up, starting from the right-angled triangle and
extending to the unit circle so that every angle, positive, negative, or
larger than a full turn, is covered.

are defined in the
right-angled triangle using opposite side, adjacent side, and hypotenuse.
You can read off the values of sine and cosine on the

for
standard angles and know how to convert between degrees and radians.

You know the key properties of sine and cosine: domain, range,
period

children.value: Trigonometric functions in the right-angled triangle

children.label: Trigonometric functions in the right-angled triangle

children.value:
Imagine a connecting rod in an engine. One end is fixed at the crankshaft
center and the other traces a circular path. To find the horizontal and
vertical components of the rod’s position at a given angle, we decompose its
length into two perpendicular directions. This decomposition is exactly what
sine and cosine do.
Consider a right-angled triangle with an acute angle

children.html: α\alphaα

children.value:
at one corner.
The three sides of the triangle are named relative to the angle

children.html: α\alphaα
α\alphaα

children.value: is the side that forms one of the two legs of

children.html: α\alphaα

children.value: is the longest side, opposite the right angle.

children.alt:
A right-angled triangle with angle \alpha, opposite side a, adjacent side
b, and hypotenuse c.
(Source: own figure; license CC BY-SA
4.0)

children.html: α\alphaα
aaa

ccc

children.value: We define the three basic trigonometric functions by the ratios of these sides:

children.html: sin⁡(α)=oppositehypotenuse,cos⁡(α)=adjacenthypotenuse,tan⁡(α)=oppositeadjacent.\sin(\alpha) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}, \quad \tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}.sin(α)=hypotenuseopposite​,cos(α)=hypotenuseadjacent​,tan(α)=adjacentopposite​.

children.value:
. Sine and cosine decompose a length into its components along
two perpendicular directions.

This triangle-based definition has one limitation: it only works for angles
between

children.html: 0°0°0°
90°90°90°

children.value:
, since a right-angled triangle cannot contain an angle
greater than

children.html: 90°90°90°

children.value:
. To describe a full rotation of the crankshaft, including
angles beyond

children.html: 90°90°90°

children.value:
and even multiple complete turns, we need a broader
definition.
We extend the definition of sine and cosine to all angles using the
, a circle of radius 1 centered at the origin. For any angle

children.value:
In engineering applications, the oscillation we want to describe is rarely a
pure sine with amplitude 1 and no phase offset. An alternating voltage, for
instance, has a specific amplitude, a specific frequency, and a specific phase
relative to some reference signal. The

children.html: f(t)=Asin⁡(ωt+φ)+df(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \varphi) + df(t)=Asin(ωt+φ)+d

children.value: captures all of these features through four parameters.

children.html: AAA

children.value:
the maximum deviation from the center value. The function
oscillates between

children.html: d−∣A∣d - |A|d−∣A∣
d+∣A∣d + |A|d+∣A∣

children.value:
. For a household alternating
voltage in Europe,

children.html: A≈325 VA \approx 325~\text{V}A≈325 V
ω\omegaω

children.value:
controls how fast the oscillation repeats.
The period is

children.html: T=2π/ωT = 2\pi/\omegaT=2π/ω

children.value: . The ordinary frequency in Hz is

children.html: f=1/T=ω/(2π)f = 1/T = \omega/(2\pi)f=1/T=ω/(2π)
f=50 Hzf = 50~\text{Hz}f=50 Hz
ω=100π rad/s\omega = 100\pi~\text{rad/s}ω=100π rad/s
φ\varphiφ

children.value: shifts the entire curve horizontally. A positive

children.html: φ\varphiφ

children.value: shifts the curve to the left by

children.html: φ/ω\varphi/\omegaφ/ω

children.value:
. When two signals
have the same frequency but different phase shifts, we say they are

. Phase differences are critical in the analysis of electrical
circuits and mechanical vibrations.

children.html: ddd

children.value:
shifts the entire curve up or down. For a pure AC
signal,

children.html: d=0d = 0d=0

children.value:
. For a signal with a DC component superimposed on an
oscillation,

children.html: d≠0d \neq 0d=0

The trigonometric functions extend the triangle definitions of sine, cosine,
and tangent to all real angles via the unit circle. Sine and cosine share the
domain

children.html: R\mathbb{R}R
[−1,1][-1, 1][−1,1]
2π2\pi2π

children.value:
, but differ in
symmetry: cosine is even, sine is odd. The tangent has a smaller domain due to
its vertical asymptotes and period

children.html: π\piπ

children.value:
. The general sinusoidal form with its
four parameters is the standard model for periodic signals in engineering. In
the next section we study the relationships between the trigonometric functions,
including the Pythagorean identity and the addition theorems, which are the
main algebraic tools for working with these functions.
